transcript.text: Hello, hello. This is a request video from Andy Sullivan. He wanted to say, he wanted to know,
Hello there Mr. Pounds, how are you? I'm good. Next video request. Can you please talk about
The differences between first world, second world, and third world countries? Also, which
Ones do you prefer the most? Why? Thanks and have a nice day. Okay. I think I've done this
Video already, but it wasn't a request video and it was like four years ago. Difference
Between first, second, and third world countries. I think the main difference, well, the main
Difference is the economy. First world countries have the most, people make the most amount of
Money and they have a, you know, better infrastructure, their roads are nicer and they have the money
To, you know, mow their lawns and all that kind of stuff. People are busy working and it's
An economy that's going, less unemployment. Um, and then third world countries are kind
Of countries that are more, uh, closer to indigenous societies, closer to primitive tribal type of
Societies like Africa. So I think there's, there's around like 200, there's like 211 countries
In the world and, um, rounding out like the bottom 50 are all African countries. And then
The other, and so those would be the third world countries. And then, and then South, a South
Asian and Southeast Asian would be some of the other third world countries like India and
Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, um, Indonesia. Second world would be, well, in third world also
Would be some of the middle, middle, uh, central America like Haiti. Uh, and then some of the, maybe
South America like Bolivia. I know, um, Honduras. And then second world would be like, um, Australia,
Chile, um, the Balkans, uh, Southeast Asia, um, Korea. Although Korea has been doing a lot better.
They may be up to, up near like first world now, but, and then first world would be America,
Australia, New Zealand, Western Europe. And then yeah, Eastern Europe would be kind of like second
World. So, um, I've, I've been around all those and seen all of them. Um, you know, the economy,
The reasons why some countries have better economy than others is, uh, can be complicated. I don't think,
I mean, I don't have it all figured out, but, but, um, I don't think anybody does, but I think the
Main reason why first world countries, and you can, well, people are welcome to correct me if they
Want, but first world countries are, uh, have better economies because they, uh, they follow the rule of
Law more. They have a long, and, and look, history comes in to play a lot, you know, precedents,
Legacy. It takes a long time for countries to develop the rule of law where people have like
A constitution and laws that have been around and people are used to following them and all of the
Different social structures, the way people do business and the laws and everything are set in
Place and people follow them, which allows people to, uh, to not have to spin their wheels or waste
Their time. Um, like, you know, spinning too many, many of their resources with security and, and, um,
And, uh, just not having it organized, you know, the better society is organized, the better it's
Going to do. Um, and, uh, you know, the more they have, the more educated the people are, uh, with
Technology. Um, you know, there are a lot of countries in the world, like Nepal, third world
Countries where their, their technology is just not, and their understanding of the people just is not
There. I was in Nepal, like in 97 or something, and I was standing in line in the immigration.
There's this American woman there and I was chatting with her and she said that she was there, uh, up in
The mountains teaching the Nepalese how to use birth control. And, uh, she said she was there a year
Beforehand and she showed them how to use condoms. And in order to show them how to use condoms, she put
The condoms on the, uh, the branches, unroll, unraveling them on the thick branches. And, and she thought
They, they understood what she was talking about and she came back a year later and she's
Like, so have you guys been using the condoms? And they're like, yeah, yeah, look, look at
The tree. And all the trees had condoms like rolled on the, on the branches. They thought
It was some kind of like, like, uh, sacred witch ceremony or something, you know? So they
Don't know shit. And like us, like Iraq, um, after we killed Saddam Hussein and put our military
In there, there was a, I think a British businessman somehow sold $80 million worth of, I saw this
On vice of, um, like bomb detectors. And all it was, was this piece of plastic with this
Antenna screwed on it. And the antenna was supposed to point to, uh, to the bomb and you, and you
Put different cards in it to detect different bombs. And they were just different plastic things
That had different things written on it. It was totally false, but he somehow went in
There and maybe he's a good salesperson and he sold them on it because they didn't have
Any understanding. Like, you know, so now some people might say, well, there must be genetically
Retarded, but I don't think it's that. Um, when you're living in a world where nobody, there's
Not enough educated people around, you don't really have a chance of becoming educated.
And then, you know, there's countries where most of the people in the country are like that.
And so the people in power are just another stupid person. They don't know anything, you
Know? And that's why we have so many terrorists, you know, like the Taliban and, uh, Afghanistan.
These people are just incredibly ignorant and, uh, they don't, I mean, they're probably sit
Around. I read a book about third world people, I think in Saudi Arabia sitting around the campfire.
They're, this is like in the seventies debating whether or not the world was flat, was flat
Around because they had heard that the Americans thought that the world was round, but they
Didn't know. And they were trying, you know, they probably had their token scientist guy
Who believed in them. The other people were like, no, no, in Quran, it says not or something
Like that. So, you know, um, you know, they think if they kill Americans, they'll get their
70 versions and stuff. So that's one thing that I noticed from going to third world countries
Is how like uneducated people are. I had a friend and he was really smart. He was a good
Carpenter. He could do stuff that I couldn't and he hung out a lot and he was illiterate
And he thought that, uh, he thought that, um, the Vatican was in America. And I asked my
Wife's brother's girlfriend to draw a picture of South Africa, America. And she drew like this
Round thing and she put Santiago on the Eastern side, like we're real. So yeah, I mean, yeah,
People in those countries are very, very, very uneducated. Um, and so, you know, if you
Want to educate them, it kind of takes, it'll take a couple of generations. You can't get
People educated just in one generation because people are formed in what they think by their
Parents. And if their parents aren't educated, then their kids aren't going to be educated,
You know, and then their kids aren't going to be educated. So it takes, you know, like five
Generations or something. I mean, just look at black people and their accents. I'm not even
Going to talk about their level of education. I don't really know that much because I haven't
Met them any, but you can tell if somebody is black in America just from their accent,
You know? So there's, and it doesn't even matter what part of America. So, um, and you,
You know, you can tell even what somebody believes that whether or not in America, even in America,
Whether like just from their accent or like simple stuff that they say or what religion they
Are. So people, there's still like all these different kinds of people in the world. And
And that are the way they are because of their environment and, uh, takes a long time for people
To change. So, so, uh, you know, I mean, now we live in a world where everybody's got the internet
And TV and you'd think that we're in this, in this modern world, but a lot of the, you know,
Before we invented planes and everybody lived around all over the world and most people didn't
Travel at all. And it'd take you forever just to get to Europe. A lot of those, uh, most of those,
Uh, um, um, you know, qualities of people and differences between people still exist to this
Day and a lot in a large part. So, so the main difference between first, first world and second
World and third world countries is that the first world countries, we, you know, our legacy
Is England and, you know, the Magna Carta and, uh, presidential, um, republics. And, you
Know, even though England is a kingdom, we've had the prime minister has been more powerful
Than the king, you know, since Robert Wadpole in like the early 1700s. So, um, there's just
The, the wealth, the, the decision-making process is more spread out and, uh, educated in England,
You know, and, uh, and then America, we're, we're even more democratic. And so we have that legacy
To fall back on. Other countries don't, um, you know, like South America, they have the income
System as their legacy and they're incredibly, uh, racially divided and economically divided.
And, uh, because of that, the poor people are going to be extremely ignorant and the rich people
Are going to be extremely ignorant in the way that they're stuck in the old world. And, uh,
And even, even their knowledge, their scientific knowledge of stuff is backwards, you know,
Like they don't think aliens are here. And, um, so because of people not following the rule of law
In these second and third world countries, it, that causes them to not have a lot of money and
It causes them to not follow laws. I mean, you, like when we live in America, you know, we see,
I mean, more or less we see, you know, if, if you do a job, you get paid, you know, if you charge
Somebody, they'll pay you. If somebody says something to you, it's true. And, uh, you know,
If you elect somebody in office, they're going to stay in office. We don't have coups, but if you look
Around the world, all these other countries have coups like violent. I mean, imagine if,
If Fox news guys decided that they didn't like, I mean, they're talking about impeach Obama,
But they can't do anything. Imagine if they said impeach Obama. And then tomorrow they actually
Showed up with their guns and they actually kicked him out. I mean, that would be like
Terrifying to America and we, that would never happen, but that happens in other countries all
The time. It happened in Chile and like 73 with, uh, when, uh, Pinochet kicked out a Linde,
I think that, and there were, you know, civil war and in Argentina in the eighties between,
You know, the politicians and, and then, and then, so, I mean, and we have crime in America,
But not on the level of these other countries, like in Argentina and Chile, they murdered 30,000
People just because they were on the opposite opposing party. And, um, and other countries don't
Have freedoms like America either. People weren't allowed to go outside after eight o'clock at night
And Chile during the seventies and this kind of thing. Um, so yeah, but I've, I mean, I've got
My little stories too. It's like, like, uh, I was in, um, I was in Nepal and we ordered,
We were at this restaurant and we ordered, uh, I ordered a burrito, right? Burrito. Ooh, cool burrito.
And then my friend ordered something and somebody else ordered something else. And we were the only,
The only, uh, customers there. And it was these, it was like three of these like young, early twenties
People who didn't really seem to speak English. And they took our order and we were sitting there,
You know, we were hanging out a bunch of different tourists. So we weren't really in a rush, but
We were there for like three hours and they never brought the food out. We started to wait and we
Went back and asked them what was going on because we were the only people there. We're like, what's going
On? And finally, I think we asked them and they finally brought the food out. And my burrito was
White wonder bread with really thin, like barely any, barely any, any, any refried beans smeared on
It. But it had been, it looked like it had been put there like two or three hours beforehand because
It was totally soggy and it was cold. And that was it. And, and my, my friend got something else.
It was just as ridiculous. I can't believe, I can't remember what it was. And like my
Friends, they freaked out. This English guy was like, I'm not paying for this. And somebody
Just says, I'm not even eating this. And some girl was like, I'm eating it. I'm dying
Of hunger. I'm going to eat what I have, even though I can barely eat it. It was some kind
Of like soup where it was just like cold water with like just pieces of like vegetable thrown
In or something. And I ate mine and it was hard to eat, but I ate it. And, and, uh, they
Didn't say anything, the people who worked there and that kind of thing.
Um, I got a salad in Bolivia at a restaurant and like half of it was lettuce. And the other
Half was, was, uh, um, onion and I could barely eat it. I mean, if you ever eat a salad where
Half of what you're eating is onion, it'll burn your mouth up. I could barely eat. I was
In excruciating pain, but that's what they called a salad. So yeah, that kind of stuff.
Um, and then of course the professionalism, they don't like in Argentina, I was there in
The, in 2001 where they defaulted on their loan. And, uh, I got a job teaching English
And I taught him this for two months and they never paid me. And I thought that they would.
So I told him that I would go and travel around Brazil, other countries and come back in like
A month and a half. So I did that and I came back and they still hadn't paid me. So I was
Like, all right, well I would. So I just stayed there three weeks calling them every couple of
Days until they finally gave me a check, but I didn't look at the date. And when I finally looked
At the date, it was post dated for two months after that. And then the check that it was for
Was for this bank that didn't even exist anymore because the, there were these two brothers who
Owned it. This is what the guy who I was trying to, at some other place, I guess the bank said he
Was, uh, yeah, there's two brothers who owned it. One of the brothers was in prison. The other one
Had disappeared with $200 million like cash. And, and, you know, and there were people protesting
In the streets, banging on pots and pans because they were, and they were, and they couldn't take
Any money out of the bank. So they were all like, there were people standing in line all the way
Around the whole block, like two lengths of the block down the block. And then down the other side
Of the block just to try to get their money out. And, uh, you know, people would have to get like
All of their family members signed up to the account and they'd all stand in line, taking
Turns, standing in line to try to get all their money out before the devaluation happened. Cause
They devalued like 350%. They were pegged to the dollar. And then it was like three and a half
Pesos to the dollar after it. And, uh, just this kind of stuff. And this was like when I was there,
You know? So, um, oh, and yeah, well, one thing, one big difference between second and third world
Countries and first world countries is people in second, third world countries, like telling
The truth doesn't really mean anything. Like they'll, they'll say anything. I was an Indonesian
And some dude wanted to be a tour guide for me at the mosque. And he said he was, uh, Bill Clinton's
Tour guide. And he said there were thousands of people there praying at this church every five
O'clock every morning. And, and then at the end he said, uh, yeah, he said, I had to like pay him
And pay the guard and all this stuff. And, oh yeah, they'll also try to chinch you out of money too.
They'll try to, they'll tell you one price and then you'll do it. And then they'll give you
Another price even higher. They think, and I guess it's true, but they think like tourists
Are like so loaded that they don't even care about money. And they'll, and so they'll lie
And they'll just, but they'll get money out of you by lying. And I, uh, um, you know, like
Borrowing money from you and then not paying you back. And I've experienced that a bunch
Of times. I'm pretty gullible, but, uh, oh, and another thing you'll notice about third
World countries is they sit around a lot. Like the unemployment is so high that people,
Like time doesn't exist to them. Like nobody's in a hurry. I'm talking about the people who
Are unemployed. I'm not talking about the rich people, like the rich people in every country
In the world, every third world country in the world, they're just like the rich people
In America, you know? So if you would get Mitt Romney to go do some, or like some oil lobbyist
Or whatever, to go do some deal with the oil lobbyist or whatever from any, you name it,
Any third world country, they're pretty much the same people, same, you know, they live
In the same kind of houses. Only difference is the one in the third world, the person
In the third world country has got like an army of people waiting, waiting on them at
Their house. Kind of like it's the South of America in slavery times. And they're dressed
In the, uh, the maid outfits. Like here in America, you'd have people clean your house
And stuff, but they're not in the outfit. Like in Chile, you would have, I was teaching
This for this lady when her husband died, but she had, I think three kids and she didn't
Have a job, but she had two people who were there all day, every day, like waiting on them.
And they were both dressed like maids. And, um, and, and, and the, the people who wait
On them aren't your friend. Like you don't talk to them. You treat, they treat them like
Workers, you know, like they'll get me a water and they, you know, go off and they're, they're
Just standing there, you know, working. And, uh, yeah, I mean, so that's one difference in,
You know, in America, you would have like Juanita or whatever, come over and clean your house,
But then she'd go and live somewhere, somewhere else, you know, and then you would talk and
Chit chat with her about her family, but I didn't, you know, not really over there.
Um, yeah, but yeah, they sit around a lot. I, I rode my bike through, uh, Morocco back
In 97 and I was passing through one road for a bunch of miles and there were all these guys
Sitting at the bottom of, of orange trees with, with their oranges and these, these baskets
To try to sell the oranges to people driving by. And none of them, you can tell none of them
Were going to sell orange that day because they were like every hundred feet, but you
Know, you, you could feel really sad really for people who just sit around all day. But
To them, it's not as bad as you might think because to them, they can sit around all day
And not really get bored. I, I was in Nepal and there are these, um, Nepalese who come
And hang out and hang out with us. And they would just kind of like sit with us and just sit
There all day with us and not even say anything, but they'd just hang out, you know? Uh, and
My friend, Saul, the illiterate guy in Chile, he'd come over and just hang out with us all
Day, every day. Cause he didn't have a job and you know, they just, you know, just sit
There. Hey, you know, and, um, yeah, in America we have stuff to do. We have things to occupy
Our time. We have, uh, video games, television. Um, you could go out to the bar. I mean, there's
All kinds of stuff you can do. You can go bike ride around the, around the bike path or
Something, you know? But over there you don't really have anything to do. Um, so you just
Go to somebody's nice house. Like you would go to somebody's house just because they have
A nice yard and you just sit there, you know? And, uh, but since that's all, you know, you
Wouldn't be bored with it. Like an American would die of boredom doing that because we
Would be like, you know, what am I doing with my life? What's happening? I should do
Something. But, uh, yeah, if you haven't really done anything like that. So I think people who
Go to third world countries and give people stuff to do, that's, that's a great service to
Them. Um, especially when it has to do with education, setting up schools with, with the
Computers and, and, you know, giving them, setting up, you know, bike race programs or

Long. Um, I think it can cause problems in the really poor countries like, uh, Africa
Where they were just, you know, they could, you know, rebels would start armies because
A lot of people throughout history would join an army and invade a country because they
Have nothing else to do because it's like third world country type of stuff. And I think
A lot of people do that just because of that. They just want excitement and just
They'll pay them to go travel around and shoot people and cool. Um, yeah, but, um, but
Yeah, um, yeah, people don't really follow the rule of law as much. I mean, it's easier
To get a job under the table in another country, but of course, you know, well in Chile, for

Thumb, you're going to get underpaid. They'll pay you less. Um, I did it. I did a classes

Classes with somebody and with somebody's kid. And when she paid me and I, and I actually
Undercharged her a lot, like I didn't charge because like the girl called me to go there

When I should have, but I didn't. Anyways, when the lady paid me, she didn't even pay

You know, where the, where the tradition would be to tip somebody in a country like that, the

And, um, yeah, and they have, and they have, there's a lot of animosity also between third

They're, uh, they don't really follow the rule of law and, um, yeah. And they'll cut,

To, even if it's just a normal worker to go and like do some construction on your house
Instead of bringing a power tool with, with the, the prongs that come out that you plug

With electricity and they'll chop a chunk of plastic off it. And then they'll wrap their

Countries that, that have so much wealth discrepancy and, um, uh, and so little people
Really knowing how to say function, operate a business that can go smoothly. For example,

Difference between people in the third world country, people in the first world country.
It's just that people are a product of their environment big time. And it takes a long time.

Countries, um, kids at the public schools, they all have to wear uniforms because, you know,
Some people are, have a little bit more money than others. And so they would dress better.
And, and, and I mean, to them dressing better and looking like you're richer and being richer,

Um, you know, would feel outcast. And, um, so they, they'll have to wear, wear, um, the, uh,
